The first and best way for you to find stories in your everyday life is something that I call homework for life. This is going to be the most important thing that I teach you throughout this entire course. In fact, if you stop listening to the course after this particular video and you move on with your life, you will have already done a great job if you begin doing homework for life. It is transformational in the way that I view my life today and it's transformational in the way thousands, tens of thousands of people around the world view their lives today. I'm about to give you a homework assignment that you will do for the rest of your life. And it is no exaggeration to say that every single day of my life, I receive an email, a tweet, a Facebook message from someone around the world who has begun to do homework for life and is emailing me or writing to me to report to me that it's changed their lives. So I don't want you to think as I begin talking about this process that I am special in some way, that this is singularly effective just for me. It is something that everyone who does finds enormous meaning and I want that for you too. So homework for life. When I started telling stories years ago, I started to get worried that I would run out of stories to tell. I didn't want to be one of those storytellers who sort of rolled out the same old chestnut every night. Those storytellers exist. I go to New York City, I start, someone starts to tell a story and I go, I've heard this one 14 times before. I don't want to be that person. I kind of want to be the person that every single time I take a stage, I'm telling a new story. Every time I go to dinner with friends, I have something new to say. Every time I'm meeting a client, I have a new idea, a new thought from my life that I can now contribute to the meeting. I always want to have new content. And so here's what I did. I gave myself this homework assignment first. I'm an elementary school teacher. I'm inclined to give homework. It's my natural instinct. I gave this to myself first. Here's what I decided. At the end of every day, before I go to bed, I'm going to reflect back upon my day and I'm going to choose the most story worthy moment from that day and I'm going to write it down. Truthfully, the actual prompt I use in my mind is this. Someone has kidnapped my family, my wife and children. And they refuse to give them back until I take a stage and tell a story about something that happened over the course of the day, even if nothing story worthy has happened over the course of the day. I must find the most story worthy moment of all the unstory worthy moments of the day. And that is the one that I will record. Now, I don't want you to write the whole story out. I don't do that either. That's insanity. Those are the kinds of people who journal, right? They tend to journal a great deal right after a breakup, right? They're at home and they're heartbroken and they fill pages of their journal. And then suddenly they find love again and the journal sort of goes back into the drawer for the next heartbreak. I don't want you to be that kind of a person. I want homework for life to be equivalent to brushing your teeth. I want it to be short, easy and you would never skip it. If you're not skipping the brushing of your teeth, which you should not do, you're not going to skip homework for life either. So the way I do it is this, take a look at this worksheet. This is a sample of a worksheet from way back in 2015. You can see it's two columns. This is not complicated. There's a date column. I record the date and then I decide not to move either. I make the decision there that I'm going to stand on this corner with this dog as long as she wants to be here because it might be the last time we're ever out in the rain together. And so I stand there for a while with her and then something else miraculous happens, something that'll happen to you if you begin doing homework for life. What happens is we start to look through our lives and through that lens of storytelling, we sort of crack open and suddenly those memories from the past, the ones we left behind, the ones we never bothered to pick up, they're going to come back to us. You're going to remember things from your past that you can't believe you ever forgot. And so on that corner that night in the rain, I look down at Kaylee, but now I don't see Kaylee anymore. I see Measelman, my very first dog, the dog that my father named after the doctor who gave him his vasectomy, Dr. Measelman. When my parents get divorced, when I'm about seven years old, my mother essentially throws my father out the house. My father's a cowboy. He grows up raising horses, riding horses. He's a black Smith who puts horseshoes on horses. But when my mother throws my father out, he loses the barn that he built with his own hands on the land he bought from his own father. And he loses the ability to have horses anymore. He also loses his kids because my mother quickly replaces my father with another man. And my father leaves my life for almost all of my life. I barely know him today. My father loses his wife and his horses and his land and his children. But standing there that night on the corner in the rain, it occurs to me for the first time, he also lost his dog, Measelman, because Measelman stayed with us when my father left. And somehow, standing on that corner that night, I realize what my father has lost through the eyes of a man for the first time, rather than through the eyes of a boy. All of that happens because I'm doing homework for life and I'm taking the time to notice the moments of my life. And then just as quickly, that memory is washed aside, and it's a new memory, suddenly coming back to me. I'm on the 18th green at Walpole Country Club. I am naked and I'm with a girl named Jen. And we're fooling around when the sprinklers come on, dumping more water onto us than I could ever imagine. And I think because I'm practically naked on Main Street and I'm having water dumped on me, now this memory has returned to me. A memory of a fun time I had with a girl when I was a teenager on a golf course. A memory I had completely forgotten until this moment on this corner. All of that happens that night because I'm doing homework for life. And it can happen for you too. And it ends up being a really important night of my life, because it's the last night Kaylee and I ever walk in the rain. She passes away two months later. But the beauty of storytelling is, I have now been without my best friend for seven years. But for a minute, I stopped staring at a camera. I stopped being in this room. For a minute, I was back on a corner in the rain. And I could see my friend just as clear as day. She came right back to me because that's the beauty of storytelling. We get to bring back the things that we've left behind and spend a little time with them again. I'm so happy I was able to just spend a little bit of time with my best friend, who I miss every day. That is the power of homework for life. Every day we'll have meaning for you. You won't go around the sun for a year and have nothing to say about it. You'll have more stories than you know what to do with. I want you to start homework for life today. Now, the problem is, some of you will not. You'll have two problems. I'm not remembering about naked romps on golf courses. This doesn't work for me. It takes time. It is not a magic pill. Homework for life requires you to believe that eventually it will make a difference in your life. If I told you it's going to take six months, but after six months you're going to see your life in a whole new way and you're going to capture moments and have more stories to tell than time to tell them, you do it. So don't give up after six days or six weeks. Persist. Trust me. It takes time to develop that lens, but once you do, you'll see that your life is filled with stories. You're not special. You're not a unicorn. You're not the one person on the planet who is not living an interesting life. You're not the one person on the planet who doesn't have stories to tell. And you may find yourself doing that too. I use my phone when someone says something or I see something. All of those moments I'm describing, these homework for life moments, whenever they happen, if I can, I open up my laptop and add them to the spreadsheet or I add them to my phone. But I do make it a practice of every day at the end of the day, entering in anything that I've recorded on my phone, plus I take a moment to reflect upon my day for anything that maybe happened that I didn't think about in the moment or didn't have time in the moment to record. My message here being you might want to start recording things throughout the day. We forget things all the time. We forget everything that we do over the course of time. The other thing I want to talk to you about is reviewing your homework for life, because I think this is important too. The way that I've set up my homework for life is using Excel, a spreadsheet format, because I like to manipulate the data. I like to keep all of my entries chronologically based upon date, but I like to be able to cut and paste them as well and move them around to different sheets, depending on what I'm doing. It works well for me. But here's what I do that I want you to think about doing. Regardless of how you're going to record your homework for life, whether it's with a pen and pencil, which is fine, or on a spreadsheet, I have lots of programmers who create apps for themselves where they enter in their homework for life on an app and it sends it directly to a spreadsheet or a Word document, whatever you choose to do. It's important that you keep it short. You can't allow yourself to write a story every day. It's got to be something that does not take a lot of time. That way you will do it every single day for the rest of your life. But what I do is I allow myself to go about 100 entries before starting a new spreadsheet. Now that's not 100 days because I average four or five entries per day, but I get 100 entries in and then I start a new spreadsheet. And the new spreadsheet has 100 entries and so on and so on. I do this because I want to go back and review my homework for life, but I want to give myself a little bit of breathing room. So I allow myself to get a couple of 100 entries away before I go back and start looking for stories. And what happens when I go through that process is I find stories that I knew were going to be stories the moment they were written down, but I also find stories that I now see as stories that I didn't necessarily see when I wrote them down. I also start to see those patterns in my life and I start to see those longer stories that I'm a meshed within. I start to see those as well. The message here being give yourself some time.
